    10. getRight()

Interface Either<L,R>

Type Parameters:
L - the Left value type
R - the Right value type
All Known Implementing Classes:
Left, Right
public sealed interface Either<L,R> permits Right<L,R>, Left<L,R>
A disjoint union (sum type) representing exactly one of two possible outcomes: Left<L> or Right<R>. This is NOT a "pair type". Either is a general purpose XOR type for reasoning about two mutually exclusive results.

Either features:

  • Is null safe: Left or Right can never contain a null value, Either does not expose any null value entry or exit. The entire API rejects null at every boundary: constructors, mapping functions, and suppliers all throw NullPointerException on null.
  • A bi-functor: bimap(CheckedFunction, CheckedFunction) transforms both Left and Right values independently.
  • bi-functor monadic chaining: biflatMap(CheckedFunction, CheckedFunction) chains operations that produce Either, on both Left and Right values independently.
  • A catamorphism: fold(CheckedFunction, CheckedFunction) collapses both possible states (Left and Right) into a single value.

Exception Handling:
Methods accepting a CheckedFunction or CheckedSupplier propagate checked exceptions transparently through a generic X extends Exception parameter. The compiler will only require handling if checked exceptions are explicitly declared or thrown in the lambda body. Unchecked exceptions (RuntimeException) propagate normally and require no declaration. Example of checked exception handling: 

    try {
        Either.left(7).bimap(n -> {
            if (n < 10) throw new Exception();
            return n * n;
        }, o -> o);
    } catch (Exception e) {
        // e has to be handled as it propagated from map
    }
While throwing unchecked exceptions does not require explicit handling: 
    Either.left(7).bimap(n -> {
        if (n < 10) throw new RuntimeException();
        return n * n;
    }, o -> o);
Alternative Types:
For accumulating multiple validation errors, use Validation.
For single value presence or absence handling, use Option.
Since:
1.0.0
Author:
Igor Flakiewicz
See Also:
  • Left
  • Right

  • Method Details

    • left

      static <L,R> Either<L,R> left(L value)
      Factory method for creating a new Left<L, R> instance.
      Type Parameters:
      L - The type of the object being contained.
      R - The type reference for a possible Right.
      Parameters:
      value - The object to be contained.
      Returns:
      A new Left<L, R> instance.
      Throws:
      NullPointerException - If value is null.
      See Also:
      • Left

    • right

      static <L,R> Either<L,R> right(R value)
      Factory method for creating a new RightLeft<L, R> instance.
      Type Parameters:
      L - The type reference for a possible Left.
      R - The type of the object being contained.
      Parameters:
      value - The object to be contained.
      Returns:
      A new RightLeft<L, R> instance.
      Throws:
      NullPointerException - If value is null.
      See Also:
      • Right

    • bimap

      <S, T, X1 extends Exception, X2 extends Exception> Either<S,T> bimap(CheckedFunction<? super L, ? extends S, ? extends X1> leftMapper, CheckedFunction<? super R, ? extends T, ? extends X2> rightMapper) throws X1, X2
      The bi-functor operation of Either. Transforms the right value using the provided rightMapper function, or the left value using the provided leftMapper function.

      When this is a Right then the rightMapper is applied to the contained value, and contained within a new Right<S, T>. When this is a Left then the leftMapper is applied to the contained value, and contained within a new Left<S, T>, allowing for convenient chaining.

      The rightMapper and leftMapper don't have to be concerned with handling null contained values as Right.value() or Left.value() can never be null.

      Checked exceptions thrown by rightMapper or leftMapper are propagated, see Either class documentation for details.

      Type Parameters:
      S - The Left type being contained after mapping.
      T - The Right type being contained after mapping.
      Parameters:
      leftMapper - The mapping function to apply to the contained value, if this is a Left.
      rightMapper - The mapping function to apply to the contained value, if this is a Right.
      Returns:
      New Right<S, T> if this is a Right. New Left<S, T> if this is a Left.
      Throws:
      X1 - If the leftMapper throws a checked exception, it is propagated to the caller.
      X2 - If the rightMapper throws a checked exception, it is propagated to the caller.
      NullPointerException - If leftMapper or rightMapper is null during lazy evaluation. Or if either mapping returns a null.
      See Also:
      • Right.bimap(CheckedFunction, CheckedFunction)
      • Left.bimap(CheckedFunction, CheckedFunction)

    • biflatMap

      <S, T, X1 extends Exception, X2 extends Exception> Either<S,T> biflatMap(CheckedFunction<? super L, ? extends Either<S,T>, ? extends X1> leftMapper, CheckedFunction<? super R, ? extends Either<S,T>, ? extends X2> rightMapper) throws X1, X2
      The bi-functor monadic chaining operation of Either. Transforms the right value using the provided rightMapper function, or the left value using the provided leftMapper function.

      This method is similar to bimap(CheckedFunction, CheckedFunction), but the mapping functions are ones whose result is already an ? extends Either<S, T>, and if invoked, biflatMap does not wrap it within an additional Either.

      If either mapping returns a null then this method throws a NullPointerException. Each mapper must always return a ? extends Either<S, T>.

      The rightMapper and leftMapper don't have to be concerned with handling null contained values as Right.value() or Left.value() can never be null.

      Checked exceptions thrown by rightMapper or leftMapper are propagated, see Either class documentation for details.

      Type Parameters:
      S - The Left type being contained after mapping.
      T - The Right type being contained after mapping.
      Parameters:
      leftMapper - The mapping function to apply to the contained value, if this is a Left.
      rightMapper - The mapping function to apply to the contained value, if this is a Right.
      Returns:
      New Either<S, T> returned by the leftMapper or the rightMapper.
      Throws:
      X1 - If the leftMapper throws a checked exception, it is propagated to the caller.
      X2 - If the rightMapper throws a checked exception, it is propagated to the caller.
      NullPointerException - If leftMapper or rightMapper is null during lazy evaluation. Or if either mapping returns a null.
      See Also:
      • bimap(CheckedFunction, CheckedFunction)
      • Right.biflatMap(CheckedFunction, CheckedFunction)
      • Left.biflatMap(CheckedFunction, CheckedFunction)

    • fold

      <T, X1 extends Exception, X2 extends Exception> T fold(CheckedFunction<? super L, ? extends T, ? extends X1> leftMapper, CheckedFunction<? super R, ? extends T, ? extends X2> rightMapper) throws X1, X2
      The catamorphism operation of Either. Collapses both possible states (Left and Right) into a single value.

      This method is similar to bimap(CheckedFunction, CheckedFunction), but forgoes the re-containing of the mapping results, and returns a concrete value T instead.

      The rightMapper and leftMapper don't have to be concerned with handling null contained values as Right.value() or Left.value() can never be null.

      Checked exceptions thrown by rightMapper or leftMapper are propagated, see Either class documentation for details.

      Type Parameters:
      T - The result type after mapping.
      Parameters:
      leftMapper - The mapping function to apply to the contained value, if this is a Left.
      rightMapper - The mapping function to apply to the contained value, if this is a Right.
      Returns:
      T from the leftMapper or the rightMapper.
      Throws:
      X1 - If the leftMapper throws a checked exception, it is propagated to the caller.
      X2 - If the rightMapper throws a checked exception, it is propagated to the caller.
      NullPointerException - If leftMapper or rightMapper is null during lazy evaluation. Or if either mapping returns a null.
      See Also:
      • bimap(CheckedFunction, CheckedFunction)
      • Present.fold(CheckedFunction, CheckedSupplier)
      • Empty.fold(CheckedFunction, CheckedSupplier)

    • swap

      Either<R,L> swap()
      Swaps the left and right values into their opposite container, preserving the type information.

      Right<L, R> becomes a Left<R, L>, conversely Left<L, R> becomes a Right<R, L>. Example: 

          Either<Integer, String> expected = Either.left(8);
    Either<String, Integer> other = Either.right(8);
    assert expected.equals(other.swap());

      swap is an involution, applying it twice returns the original: 

          assert either.swap().swap().equals(either);
      Returns:
      A new Either with left and right swapped, Either<R, L>.

    • isLeft

      boolean isLeft()
      Is this a Left.
      Returns:
      true if this is a Left, otherwise false
      See Also:
      • isRight()

    • isRight

      boolean isRight()
      Is this a Right.
      Returns:
      true if this is a Right, otherwise false
      See Also:
      • isLeft()

    • getLeft

      Option<L> getLeft()
      Projects this into an instance of Option.

      When this is a Left, the result is a new Present representation of Option, preserving the contained value and the type L. Otherwise, returns a new Empty only preserving the type constraint L.

      This is a more terse and intention revealing equivalent to doing a fold(CheckedFunction, CheckedFunction) into an Option: 

          Either<Integer, String> original = Either.left(8);
    Option<Integer> folded = original.fold(Option::of, _ -> Option.empty());
    assert original.getLeft().equals(folded);
      Returns:
      If this is a Left, returns a Present<L>. Otherwise, returns a Empty<L>.
      See Also:
      • Option
      • getRight()

    • getRight

      Option<R> getRight()
      Projects this into an instance of Option.

      When this is a Right, the result is a new Present representation of Option, preserving the contained value and the type R. Otherwise, returns a new Empty only preserving the type constraint R.

      This is a more terse and intention revealing equivalent to doing a fold(CheckedFunction, CheckedFunction) into an Option: 

          Either<String, Integer> original = Either.right(8);
    Option<Integer> folded = original.fold(_ -> Option.empty(), Option::of);
    assert original.getRight().equals(folded);
      Returns:
      If this is a Right, returns a Present<R>. Otherwise, returns a Empty<R>.
      See Also:
      • Option
      • getLeft()
